mappings.rationaleAI: Megaliths are monumental artistic and architectural creations of Neolithic and Bronze Age societies, serving burial, ritual, and ceremonial purposes across Europe, Asia, and Africa, directly illustrating the arts of Stone Age and prehistoric peoples.

Greek mythology from Homer and Hesiod through Hellenistic and Roman adaptations constitutes a foundational artistic tradition of Western antiquity (B.1), while its enduring reinterpretation in modern Western literature, art, and philosophy extends its relevance to Renaissance and modern European arts (B.3).

Traces the Western poetic tradition from Sappho's lyric verse through Hellenistic Alexandria to Roman love elegy by Catullus, illustrating how ancient Greek and Roman artistic expression in poetry evolved across classical antiquity.

Aesop's fables represent a foundational literary genre of ancient Greek popular culture, combining moral instruction with narrative art and transmitting across centuries of the Western literary tradition.

Directly examines the evolving social identity and status of artists across Western cultural history, from Greek and Roman antiquity through Renaissance workshop culture and patronage to Romantic conceptions of artistic genius, spanning the full arc of the Western artistic tradition.

Traces artistic depictions of the underworld across Western culture from ancient Egyptian and Greco-Roman literature (Homer, Virgil) through medieval art (Dante's Inferno, Bosch's paintings) to modern literary works by Eliot, Levi, and Sartre, spanning all periods of the Western artistic tradition.

Covers the origins and development of literature as an art form from Mesopotamian and Egyptian writing through Greek literary traditions to medieval manuscript culture, tracing the emergence of poetry, epic narrative, and drama across ancient and medieval Western civilization.

Examines the pastoral literary tradition as a distinctive Western art form from Theocritus and Virgil in classical antiquity through its revival in Elizabethan English poetry, illustrating continuity of artistic genre across ancient and early modern European culture.

Byzantine culture preserved and extended classical Greek artistic and literary traditions for a millennium after Rome's fall, serving as a centre of classical learning whose cultural influence bridges ancient and medieval Western arts.

The Picts' intricately carved symbol stones featuring animals, geometric patterns, and Christian crosses represent a distinctive artistic tradition of early medieval northern Britain, illustrating arts of the Middle Ages in a specific cultural context.

The emergence of Gothic architecture as an engineering and aesthetic revolution, alongside the broader intellectual and cultural transformation of twelfth-century Western Europe through contact with Byzantine and Islamic civilizations, exemplifies arts of the medieval Western tradition.

Directly examines the revival of Greco-Roman pagan imagery in Renaissance European art, including Botticelli's Primavera and Titian's Danae, illustrating the tension between classical artistic subjects and Christian patronage that defined Renaissance artistic culture.

Raphael's fresco exemplifies Renaissance humanism's artistic synthesis of classical learning with Christian patronage, revealing how early modern European artists positioned themselves as heirs to the Greco-Roman intellectual and artistic tradition.

Germaine de Stael developed foundational ideas behind Romanticism in European arts, bridging French and German literary traditions and challenging Enlightenment rationalism in aesthetics across early nineteenth-century European culture.

Plutarch's biographical literature shaped Western artistic culture both as a foundational work of ancient Greek and Roman literary art (B.1) and through its direct influence on Shakespeare's plays and Enlightenment-era writing, transmitting classical ideals into Renaissance and modern European arts (B.3).

The Amazon myth featured prominently in ancient Greek pottery and sculpture as a major artistic subject (B.1), and its revival during the Renaissance shaped European artistic and literary culture in the early modern period (B.3).

One of the four great classical novels of Chinese literature with cultural influence across China, Japan, Korea, and Vietnam comparable to Homer's impact on the West, directly representing the literary arts of East Asian peoples.

Mughal syncretic Indo-Islamic cultural production in art, architecture (including the Taj Mahal), and literature represents a major artistic tradition of South Asia (C.3), blending Islamic and Indian artistic forms (D).

The Great Mosque of Djenne, a UNESCO-listed architectural monument surviving from the Mali Empire, represents the arts of West African and Sudanic cultures, connecting Islamic artistic influence with indigenous West African architectural traditions.
Dadaism emerged in early 20th-century Europe as a distinctive arts movement spanning performance, poetry, and visual art, belonging to the arts from the Renaissance to the present in Europe (B.3).

Dragon imagery appears across Western arts from antiquity (B.1), East Asian arts in China, Japan, and Korea (C.1), Middle Eastern and Islamic arts (D), and African peoples' arts (E), reflecting a cross-cultural artistic phenomenon.
